---
title: "Homework 4"
author: "Natasha Atkins"
format: pdf
editor: visual
---

## Define the inputs, source the function, and run on test data

```{r}
# the formula I'm using: D = sum(n/N)^2
# ni = the total number of organisms of species i
# N = total number of organisms of all species
# D is the result, which is simpsons diversity, which tells the probability that two individuals randomly selected from a sample will belong to the same species; closer to 0 means infinite diversity and closer to 1 means no diversity
# To counter act the counterinuitiveness, we can do 1-D
# 1-D = the probability that two individuals randomly selected from a sample will belong to different species

# source the function
library(here)

source(here("diversity_function.R"))

# generate test data
set.seed(513)
species_counts <- c(
  rep("species_A", rpois(1, lambda = 32)),
  rep("species_B", rpois(1, lambda = 18)),
  rep("species_C", rpois(1, lambda = 10)),
  rep("species_D", rpois(1, lambda = 4)),
  rep("species_E", rpois(1, lambda = 2))
)
species_counts <- as.factor(species_counts)
species_counts

ni <- as.numeric(table(species_counts))  # counts per species
N <- length(species_counts)              # total individuals


# use the function
testing_function <- species_div(ni, N)
testing_function

#subtract from one to determine how diverse the sample is, probability that 2 selected individuals will be from different species.
print(1-testing_function)
```
